edges.description: Skin penetration enables larval movement within the epidermis.
Migrating larvae trigger local inflammatory responses along the track.

nodes.description: Local inflammatory reaction with eosinophils contributes to pruritic and bullous/vesicular lesions in some cases.

evidence.reference_title: Bullous cutaneous larva migrans: case series and review of atypical clinical presentations.
evidence.snippet: Cytological examinations of the blisters showed the presence of lymphocytes and neutrophils, with numerous eosinophils.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in a child: a case report and review of literature.
evidence.snippet: The larvae of Ancylostoma spp. are common culprits, causing a localized inflammatory reaction as they migrate through the skin.

nodes.description: Slowly migrating erythematous linear or serpiginous tracks are the characteristic skin lesion.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in the northeastern US.
evidence.snippet: It is characterized by erythematous, twisting, and linear plaques that can migrate to adjacent skin.

evidence.reference_title: Bullous cutaneous larva migrans: case series and review of atypical clinical presentations.
evidence.snippet: These tracks may be single or multiple, serpiginous or linear, ramified and intertwined, accompanied by pruritus.

nodes.description: Infective larvae enter through exposed skin after contact with contaminated sand or soil.

evidence.reference_title: Successful Treatment of Cutaneous Larva Migrans With Combined Albendazole and Ivermectin Therapy: A Report of Two Cases From Sudan.
evidence.snippet: Cutaneous larva migrans (CLM), caused by third-stage filariform larvae of cat and dog hookworms, presents as pruritic, serpiginous tracks upon skin penetration by larvae from contaminated soil.

nodes.description: Larvae migrate within the epidermis, producing slowly advancing linear or serpiginous inflammatory tracks.

evidence.reference_title: Bullous cutaneous larva migrans: case series and review of atypical clinical presentations.
evidence.snippet: Hookworm-related cutaneous larva migrans (HRCLM) is caused by the penetration and migration in the epidermis of larvae of Ancylostoma braziliense and Ancylostoma caninum.

nodes.description: Albendazole is an anthelmintic alternative used for CLM, including pediatric cases.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in a child: a case report and review of literature.
evidence.snippet: Treatment options include albendazole or ivermectin, with preventive measures emphasizing hygiene, footwear use, and pet deworming.

evidence.reference_title: Bullous cutaneous larva migrans: case series and review of atypical clinical presentations.
evidence.snippet: All patients were successfully treated with oral albendazole.

nodes.description: Oral ivermectin is a first-line anthelmintic treatment; some patients require repeat doses.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in the northeastern US.
evidence.snippet: Oral ivermectin, the preferred first-line treatment for cutaneous larva migrans, was administered in combination with triamcinolone.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in travelers: a prospective study, with assessment of therapy with ivermectin.
evidence.snippet: The cure rate after a single dose of ivermectin was 77%.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in travelers: a prospective study, with assessment of therapy with ivermectin.
evidence.snippet: In 14 patients, 1 or 2 supplementary doses were necessary, and the overall cure rate was 97%.

nodes.description: Itching is a dominant symptom and major driver of morbidity and sleep disturbance.

evidence.reference_title: Life quality impairment caused by hookworm-related cutaneous larva migrans in resource-poor communities in Manaus, Brazil.
evidence.snippet: Intense pruritus, sleep disturbance (due to itching) and the feeling of shame were the most frequent skin disease-associated life quality restrictions (reported by 93.4%, 73.6%, and 64.8% of the patients, respectively).

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous larva migrans in a child: a case report and review of literature.
evidence.snippet: The lesion gradually expanded, forming a serpiginous erythema, and became intensely pruritic.
